desc:
The valley bottom is covered in rocks and debris which have broken from the
mountain and come tumbling down to end up here. The valley bends here to the
north and east, or you can climb the mountain to the west.
name: In a Valley

desc:
Rocks from above lie strewn all over the floor, some large rocks, some small.
Most look like they have been lying here for many years, ravaged by wind and
rain. The valley bends west and south; to the east a smaller valley climbs up
towards a narrow gap in the Hithaeglir.
name: In a Valley

desc:
The trail you are following leads east and west here. Westwards you can see
mountains rising to high peaks, while to the east you can see the wide valley of
the Anduin. In the far north you spot an area of darkness which may herald a
forest of some sort, but you can't be sure from this distance. Still in the
north but rather closer you see a path winding its way east to west.
name: Narrow Gap

desc:
Here is the lowest part of a slippery and steep path through the mountain. It is
only possible to travel north. Some lichens grows here but else this place is
deserted of any form of permanent life.
name: Steep Path

desc:
The valley continues north and south here. Picking your way carefully through
shattered rocks, you wonder if you will ever find a way out of here.. when
suddenly you spot a trail leading up the side of the mountain to the west. All
around you can look up and see the magnificent snow-capped peaks towering high
above you.
name: In a Valley

desc:
A rock wall towers up on the east, casting its long shadow over the valley
floor. In the west you can start to climb up the side of one of the tallest
peaks in this range, or you can follow this barren valley either north or south.
name: In a Valley

desc:
The valley ends here, dropping so sharply to the south that it would be folly to
even try going down it. You can climb the mountains to the west, where you see
the highest peak you have ever beheld rising far above your head, or you can
follow the valley northwards. To the east a tall cliff blocks your way.
name: In a Valley

desc:
Travel further south is curtailed by a large outcrop of rocks, which is almost
impossible to pass. You can however descend into a valley to the east, or climb
back up the mountain in the west.
name: On a Mountainside

desc:
Standing on the loose rocks you wonder if this was a wise move, but the ground
underfoot seems to be solid enough for the moment. You can traverse the side of
the mountain to the north or south, descend to a valley in the east, or climb up
to a ridge on the top of the mountain in the west.
name: On a Mountainside

desc:
The rocks on this side of the mountain are loose, but not loose enough to make
you lose your balance and fall. You can descend further into the valley bottom,
traverse the mountainside to the south, or climb back up to the ridge in the
north or west.
name: On a Mountainside

desc:
Standing here you are dwarfed by the size of the peak which rises in the south.
It looks impossibly steep for climbing, with many sharp rocks which could cut
your hands to shreds. You can travel west and follow the ridge of smaller peaks
which snakes out to the north-west, or you can descend the mountain to a valley
in the east.
name: On Top of a Mountain

desc:
This is one of the highest peaks in the area, and from here you can look all
around and see the Misty Mountains in all their glorious splendour. To the
north, the mountains slowly descend into a shallow valley, cut by a brown ribbon
which must be a road, running east-west. To the south the mountains close ranks,
forming an impassable barrier of rock, while to the east and west the mountains
continue on for miles in their snow-capped, mist-wreathed splendour.
name: A Very High Peak

desc:
You have now reached the bottom of the valley. A few bushes and some grass cover
the slopes, feeding the few animals that dwell here. The mountain slopes bear
footmarks of humanoid beings.
name: Vale in the Hithaeglir

desc:
Rocks again... what a surprise. The rough path you are following along the side
of this mountain makes a bend to the east and south here: to the east the path
descends to the bottom of a valley, while to the south it enters a narrow
canyon. Mountains rise up majestically in the north and west, but you can spot
no way of climbing them from here.
name: The Mountainside

desc:
You are on the bottom of a rocky canyon, about two meters wide. High, smooth
cliffs, with no handholds in sight, flank you on both sides, leaving almost no
choice on where to proceed: you can either follow the canyon to the north, or
squeeze through a crevice westwards.
name: Narrow Canyon

desc:
At the centre of a large circle, formed by some old firs, stands an ancient oak
tree. The knots on its trunk seem to form a face, as if it were grimly looking
out at the world. Its boughs rise towards the sky, like an evil priest might
raise his arms in chanting words of doom. Every now and then, as the wind blows,
the other trees sway, like a great crowd in an uproar.
name: Gathering of the Trees

desc:
The surface of the road is somewhat bumpy with small patches of grass growing
among the rough stones. If one looks carefully, traces of aged wagon tracks can
still be seen in the hard clay. The road runs from the west, where a dark forest
looms, southward to the dim outline of a mountain range on the horizon.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
The surface of the road is somewhat bumpy with small patches of grass growing
among the rough stones. If one looks carefully aged wagon tracks can be seen in
the sections covered by hard clay. The road runs from the north, where a small
river is still visible, to the south, where a mountain stands.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
The craggy ground has a rugged surface littered with numerous small rocks. A few
remaining bulky stumps are scattered across the ground giving the impression
that a forest existed here a long time ago. The ground slopes gently to the east
where the soil has fewer rocks. Bypassing this area to the south and west is an
unused-looking road.
name: Rough Plains

desc:
The road turns sharply here, skirting a gently rising slope to the south-west.
The terrain towards the east and north, which holds numerous young saplings,
contains the remains of an old forest. Further south-west a tremendous mountain
overshadows the surrounding lands.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
West of the road the ground rises sharply towards a tall mountain while the
ground slowly ascends to the south. East of the road there is a grove of young
saplings growing among the rotten tree stumps of a former forest. The road
continues both south and north.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
This area was once a vast forest but was cut down long ago. A large number of
rotten tree stumps are visible, many of them now serving as homes for termites
and grubs. Among the stumps a new forest of young saplings grows.
name: Tangled Cutting Grounds

desc:
Various kinds of slender young trees form a thick and exuberant grove here. The
soil is dank and fertile with a lot of small undergrowth struggling beneath the
trees. A few hoary stubs of large trees are scattered in sections, cut down by
woodsmen long ago. An old forest road is visible southward, being rarely used
now that the major woods have diminished.
name: Cramped Grove

desc:
The road makes a gentle turn here, continuing to the west and south. A grove
with lush vegetation is located to the north. To the east there is a large
strange-looking hollow, filled with bushes and covered by a soft mist. It
appears that this road is infrequently travelled upon from the state of the
roadbed.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
Thick bushes fill this large hollow which was formed long ago by some mystical
event of the sky. The soil here is obviously saturated because groundwater
easily rises when even the smallest of creatures walks upon it. The air is very
humid and a deep mist forms from the ground. A clearing, cloaked in haze, opens
to the north.
name: Misty Hollow

desc:
A strange-looking hollow covered with haze and thick bushes lies on the north
side of the road and is the source of the mist that covers this region. East of
here the ground descends to a large plain of rich soil. The road continues to
the south and west.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
The old forest road runs to the east and north while a muddy trail joins it from
the south. The trail has a rough surface and appears to be rarely used. To the
east the road continues until it is out of sight. A tall grass covers the ground
on the west side of the road.
name: Fork in the Road

desc:
The surface of this trail has been formed by layers of mud that have hardened
during the dry spells between rains. Depressions from wagons are barely
detectable in the battered ground. The eastern edge of the trail is overlaid
with large bushes, while the area to the west looks grassy. The trail continues
both to the north and the south.
name: Muddy Trail

desc:
Grass covers the land that borders this loam trail. Its infrequent usage seems
to be only by wandering animals. The trail declines slightly to the south where
it later turns westward. Far in the north the trail connects to a wider road
that is in better condition.
name: Muddy Trail

desc:
This plain seems very lively having an abundance of vegetation consisting of
grass several feet high and various kinds of flowers. Several small bushes with
small, green leaves have also managed to take root in the excellent soil. A
mud-covered trail to the east and south bypasses this plain.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
A few burdock bushes are scattered among the clovers that cover this placid
plain. A trail of matted grass and lumps of excrement marks the frequent passage
of large animals across these plains. An entrance to a burned village is located
southward.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The rough trail bends here continuing to the north and the west where the
remains of a burned village stand. It appears that the trail has not been used
in a long time because of the rampant growth of hay which now covers it. The
ground ascends slightly towards the east where a wide spread of grassland lies.
name: Bend in Muddy Trail

desc:
Plains covered with grass and a few bushes surround this muddy trail which rises
gently towards a burned village standing in the west. The mud on this trail
could show the tracks of animals but the settling and flowing of the mud on this
slope eventually removes them.
name: Muddy Trail

desc:
This spot, where the land rises towards the ruined yard of a burned down
village, marks the head of a muddy trail. The walls which once protected this
village from its enemies are now crumbling and the wooden gateway which stood
between the walls has been smashed. The remains of a burned out guard tower can
be found southwards.
name: Entrance to the Burned Down Village

desc:
A burned down village stands east of these dry plains. The plain appears very
barren with only a small amount of vegetation, consisting mainly of a few small
stunted bushes and some short grass. Traces of some animals can be seen on the
softer spots of the ground when they pass through.
name: Drought Stricken Land

desc:
Although the soil here is withered, small patches of grass and a few short
bushes still manage to grow. It seems that the vegetation becomes more lush to
the east, while the land to the north looks rocky and barren. Towards the south
stands what was once a sturdy stone wall, but it is now falling apart.
name: Scrubby Plains

desc:
Numerous rocks of various sizes litter the ground, forming a patchy carpet of
stone. A few parched branches squeeze their way through the rocks, reaching for
light. To the north a mountain trail declines to here. The soil softens towards
the east and south while the rocky land continues to the west.
name: Rocky Land

desc:
The terrain slopes gently down towards a pond some twenty feet wide and filled
with squalid-looking water. A few lily pads lie on the surface of the water, and
various stones, covered with caustic phlegm, form a border around the pond.
Tracks of various animals are embedded upon the muddy ground.
name: Slimy Pond

desc:
The rich soil of this land is bountiful with dense hay. During the warm summer
months various kinds of flowers burst with health, creating a richly-coloured
environment. There are some smooth little stones scattered about and several
small holes in the ground, most likely dug by moles.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Short and dry grass covers this barren plain which contains only a few small
bushes and some large rocks. Bones of some small animals are scattered around
and other traces of different kinds of animals are imprinted on the surface.
name: Drought Stricken Land

desc:
This barren land supports only a few stunted bushes and a couple of feeble
pines. Very little grass has managed to take root in this poor soil. Towards the
east the land becomes more hospitable to vegetation and more grass can be seen.
A battered stonewall stands to the south-east, separating a burned down village
from these barren lands. The region to the north appears very rugged.
name: Scrubby Lowland

desc:
The ground here is rugged and lifeless, with a few broken stones littered across
the top and an occasional dead root embedded in the surface. The bones of
several small animals can still be seen though many of them have been crushed.
To the south, where the soil appears moister, some stunted vegetation has begun
to grow but to the east the rocky land continues.
name: Rocky Land

desc:
Numerous rocks, both small and large, cover this arid ground as it nears the top
of the mountain from the south, making travel difficult and the land
inhospitable to vegetation. A small trail winds from the east on its way to the
mountain's peak. To the south the terrain appears similar to here. Rising in the
north is the peak of the mountain.
name: Steep Incline

desc:
From here, the top of the mountain, you can see much of the surrounding lands.
The southern slope gently descends, while the other sides of the mountain are
much steeper and more perilous. A small precipice is to the east, though it
appears somewhat unstable and would be difficult to reach.
name: Top of the Mountain

desc:
This side of the mountain is very steep and even an experienced climber would
find it nearly impossible to continue southward. The terrain is rough and barren
with no vegetation. Towards the south a tall mountain looms, overshadowing the
lands around.
name: Steep Mountainside

desc:
The ground here is rough and hard with numerous small stones embedded into the
surface although a few stunted, ancient junipers have managed to grow in the
softer spots. The land rises gently towards the south where it eventually forms
a steep mountain. To the north and east the remains of an old forest road can be
found.
name: Rising Slope

desc:
The road bends sharply here, continuing north and east. Southwards the ground
begins to rise towards some mountains. The surface of the road is hard packed,
indicating that it was once frequently travelled, although the small patches of
grass that are now growing make it doubtful the road has seen much use recently.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
A steep gloomy rock face rises to the south and west forcing the road to make a
sharp turn east and north of here. The southern wall of the rock face is sheer
and covered by some thick bushes while the west wall appears a bit gentler and
has a few cracks which make passage that way possible for a skilful climber.
name: Road beside the Mountain

desc:
This road is bordered by a gentle embankment of dirt and several small rocks to
the south. A tangled logging area covered with young saplings is to the north.
The road appears to be infrequently used since grass has managed to grow in many
sections.
name: Old Forest Road

desc:
Rocks of various sizes, as well as loose gravel, cover this embankment that
follows the road just north of here. There is no vegetation growing in this
austere ground for it is far too harsh to support plant life. A gloomy mountain
with a sheer face stands west and is unreachable from this direction.
name: Rocky Embankment

desc:
The ground here is particularly hard, and is unable to support much in the way
of vegetation. Only a few scraggly trees have managed to take root among the
stones that litter the ground. This rocky land rises northward and to the far
south a small pond has formed. In the east the soil becomes more fertile.
name: Stony Soil

desc:
The grass here has prospered and grown to dominate this smooth region which
gently declines eastward towards a crossing in the road. The soil becomes more
barren in the west but seems to burst with health in the eastern lands where the
river Anduin flows at its mightiest.
name: Grass beside the Road

desc:
The road descends gently eastward where the terrain is more fertile and able to
support its blooming vegetation. This poses a stark contrast with the land to
the west where the soil is harsh and the terrain is mostly barren. The surface
of the road is covered with short grass indicating it has seen little usage.
name: Bend in the Old Forest Road

desc:
The precipice is soft and brittle allowing many large fissures to form through
the passage of time. Several of the fissures have caved in over the years making
it apparent that they are both hollow and unstable. A few stunted pine trees
have managed to grow in the less harsh portions of the slope. The western
horizon is covered by a tremendous peak which obscures the view in that
direction as it rises from the ground.
name: Uneven Precipice

desc:
The head of this trail originates at the foot of the soft mountain slope, and
worms its way up to the precipice. The trail is tiled with small circular stones
and seems of an ancient origin, possibly constructed by mountain dwellers. A
short yellow grass traces the edge of the trail as it winds up towards the
precipice.
name: Winding Mountain Trail

desc:
You find yourself in the outskirts of the vast mountain forest that carpets the
valley side. The trees are more disperse in this area, opening room for a richer
and more diverse undergrowth. Especially prevalent are the tall, leafy ferns
that crowd around the thick trunks. Light filters in through the canopy of
leaves, casting whimsical patterns on the ground. Only at dusk, however, does
the forest show all its splendour when the waning light falls slanting over the
treetops and orange glints shimmer on the rims of the leaves.
name: Light Forest

desc:
The forest here thins, making way for some small bushes. The trees seem younger,
about twice as tall as an adult human, and the bushes bear small red berries
when in season. The foliage all around is thick and dark green in colour.
Resilient branches and leaves that snap back when pushed aside, creating a
confusing flurry, must slow travellers to these parts. A few hack marks made in
the plants to clear a path seems to have had no effect. The leaves are ever
growing and new leaves quickly replace the ones cut away.
name: Light Forest

desc:
In these borderlands between the forest and the vast open plains there still
grows an occasional tree among the small bushes and sturdy grasses. Westwards
the trees grow denser, while in all other directions the forest completely
vanishes.
name: Bordering Plains

desc:
A few solemn, scattered trees stand among the bushes and grasses in this area.
Like silent sentinels they seem to keep an ever vigilant watch for some unknown
menace, as if they were scouts or guardians of the forest that grows a short
distance to the north-west. Eastwards the plains open up as the Vale of the
Anduin gradually unfolds in front of you.
name: Guardian Trees

desc:
The land declines gently towards the east where a healthy, leafy brushwood
grows. The vegetation here is short, consisting mostly of grass with a few small
shrubs. The rising terrain to the west appears quite harsh while the eastern and
southern lands are more fertile.
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
Young slender trees slowly grow here, replacing those that were cut down in the
distant past. The soil is soft and nutritious offering a hospitable substratum
for the growing vegetation. The terrain southward seems to also be a former
habitat of trees and now contains many young saplings.
name: Woody Coppice

desc:
Several bountiful bushes cover this smooth region which has many kinds of
plants. The smaller plants, however, are left in the shadow of the bigger ones
and thus they struggle for life. Most of the soil appears quite fertile.
name: Brushwood

desc:
The ground here is filled with small bushes of a strange species. This type of
bush seems to grow only in this area for some mysterious reason. There are no
animal tracks in this area as if they shun these strange bushes and their slimy,
long reaching leaves. In a few places near the bushes some bones of small
animals are visible.
name: Among the Strange Bushes

desc:
The ambience of this clearing is very mysterious, with a strange mist rising
from the hollow lying to the south. The ground here is muddy and provides uneven
footing for travellers. A few puddles of water are scattered about. The horizon
is largely obscured by a strange mist.
name: Hazy Clearing

desc:
The vegetation here has a lower density when compared to the lands westward. A
few medium-sized bushes grow here, leaving fairly wide routes between them. The
ground is abundantly covered by animal tracks and even a few humanoid prints are
visible on the surface. A wide valley opens out towards the east and continues
into the far distance.
name: Sparse Bushes

desc:
Scattered on the vast plains are a few patches of low growing bushes along with
some plants and creeping vines. Otherwise this area is dominated by weeds and
grasses not strongly bound by the plants. Occasionally a light blowing wind
stirs up some dust which slowly settles back to the ground covering the
greenery.
name: Dusty Plains

desc:
The proximity of this place to the river just north-east of here has created a
rich and varied environment where various plants, weeds and grasses compete for
the mineral rich space. A few single trees stand spread out along the bank of
the river, throwing their small shadows across the otherwise open plains.
name: Lush Plains

desc:
Various plants battling for space mix here into one big tangled mass. It is hard
to tell exactly what plants make up this entanglement since there is no way to
separate them all. Clearly there are some wild grasses and herbs, along with
some wildflowers, all corralled by several groups of shrubs. Far to the west a
dense and dark mass of trees line the horizon, while to the east the plains
stretch out to the horizon.
name: Tangled Plain

desc:
These plains are a patchwork of varied vegetation. A single tree stands in a
small cluster of bushes, while a dense group of brushwood is slowly creeping in
from the south. To the northeast the vegetation slowly diminishes, giving way to
vast open areas of grass.
name: Brushy Plains

desc:
Large bushes with thick leaves grow more tightly-packed, forming a dense
cluster, because of the excellent soil. The terrain south of here is made of the
same soil and is covered in similar bushes. While eastwards the vegetation
merely thins, to the west it appears scanty.
name: Brushwood

desc:
A thin layer of slime covers the floor of this small pit beneath the dismal
chamber above. A trickle of dirty water drips from the rusty grating in the
ceiling of this pit. The ground here is soft and muddy with the water as it
slowly soaks into the soil on its way into the groundwater beneath here.
name: Watery Pit

desc:
A powerful darkness fills this circular chamber, making it difficult to discern
shadows. The floor descends slightly towards the centre of the room where a
rusty grating is set in the floor. The water flows from the tunnel south of here
in a small ditch then through the grating down into the filthy pit which sits
under this chamber.
name: Dismal Chamber

desc:
A small ditch has formed in the floor as water flows through this tunnel. The
air is very humid here and the walls are covered with mould. The roof is made of
many small bricks and has been shaped into the form of an arch. As the water
flows north it passes through a circular chamber.
name: Arched Gallery

desc:
Humid air fills this large deserted cellar which was once used as a storage area
for food. The scents of rotted food are present. The stone walls of the basement
are covered with verdigris and water trickles from the roof. The floor seems to
decline slightly northward where the flowing water has gathered. A couple of
wooden barrels stand in the south-western corner.
name: Dank Basement

desc:
These huge stone stairs appear to be worn smooth except where many small cracks
on their surface show. The cracks have been eroded from the trickle of water
formed by the condensing of the humid air. However, the stairs seem quite firm
and can probably support a person's weight.
name: Massive Stone Stairs

desc:
The entire ceiling of this great hall has collapsed, littering the floor with
stones of various sizes. Sections of the walls have also been smashed in and
several gaps are easily large enough to allow light into the room. Other rooms
once led off this one, but nothing of those rooms remains. Numerous bones jut
out from under the fallen rubble and a couple of shattered skeletons can be
seen, making this room look like a mass grave. To the north there is another
ruined hall.
name: Crumbled Hall

desc:
From the scattered stones one can tell that this huge building once underwent
some sort of large-scale catastrophe. The basic structure seems to have endured
surprisingly well apart from the fallen mezzanine and the burned furniture. Many
blocks of the former roof can be seen scattered among the skeletal remains on
the ground. To the south there lies another hall which appears to have crumbled
more extensively than this one.
name: Scorched Mansion

desc:
In the centre of this destroyed and ash-covered district stands a colossal well,
some ten feet in diameter. The ring of the well is built of large granite blocks
many of which have become loose or have fallen from the well ring. A large winch
with a frail rope and wooden bucket stands above the well, seemingly still in
workable condition.
name: At the Broken Well

desc:
The extent of the mass destruction that was inflicted upon this village years
ago is still easily visible when viewing the remains of the dwellings. However,
time and the ravages of the weather have worked at the village and have softened
some of the harshness of the destroyed structures. The strong stonewalls which
surround the village are surprisingly well preserved. The only building that
seems to have endured the ravage in some fashion is a large structure standing
in the west. To the south there lies a massive but shattered well.
name: Ruined Yard

desc:
This hidden cavern offers excellent shelter for any traveller seeking cover from
bad weather. The walls of the cave are craggy and a natural chimney rises high
above, blackened from the use of a fireplace standing in the middle of the stony
floor. Around the fireplace lie lumps of straw, offering a comfortable resting
place for weary travellers. A dense thicket covers the cave's entrance.
name: A Natural Cavern

desc:
Like the walls of an ornate tribe of beasts, the irregular walls here are
decorated with the imprints of fossilized creatures. Perhaps shaped in random
patterns to our civilized eyes, who knows what song they sing to an unknown
entity.
name: Tunnel

desc:
Branching off to the east, this long dead river continues along its north- south
course. Scattered on the floor, bits of broken deadwood and dry silt are the
only remaining contents of the river. Its history however, is clear enough to
see. While not as fierce as the rivers that carved the Burrow, it was powerful
enough to forge these caverns.
name: A Tunnel

desc:
Here, the wall's composition changes from shale and limestone back to heavy
thick granite. The river, meeting this stubborn force, was not powerful enough
to drive through the stone. Perhaps slicing 10 meters into the rock, it gave out
and instead glanced back south and to the east, travelling along the path of
least resistance.
name: A Cave

desc:
This area must of once been something similar to a pool. Accepting the pounding
water from the south, which was sent up this direction by stubborn walls of
granite, it collected here widening the natural crevice into a smoother, hollow
bowl. Tunnels lead off to the west, east and south.
name: A Cave

desc:
The tunnel, like a crazed arrow bouncing off heavy targets, darts south here. A
fierce battle with the thick granite to the east prevented any advance, and
instead, the old river cut through the soluble sulphur to the south. Now, eons
later, dry sulphur dust fills the air where once a mighty river raged.
name: A Tunnel

desc:
Allowing enough buffer between yourself and the Burrow, the airy tunnel
continues north-south. Torches and lanterns are forbidden on the walls, assuring
no peep of light could betray those travelling within this pass. Free of all
obstacles and well maintained, even the need for personal light is shunned.
name: Goblins' Pass

desc:
Reaching a peak here, the walls bank smoothly into a gentle turn, tunneling back
towards the Burrow. Scattered farther to the east are secret mechanical doors,
built within these granite walls. Using goblin mechanisms, the doors swing one
way, preventing any intruders from gaining access to this secret route.
name: Goblins' Pass

desc:
Arching like a smooth black serpent, these walls turn southerly and to the west.
The smooth floor is hidden here, lined instead with matted furs of black wargs
and brown bears. Your footsteps, muffled in the unnatural bedding barely reach
your ears before being lost within the inescapable grasp of these tunneled
walls.
name: Goblins' Pass

desc:
Here the water becomes strangely more turbulent. Peering into it, swirls of
motion can be seen from its unfathomable depths. Tiny faint bubbles float to the
surface, indicating all that may be down there is not simple harmless plant
life.
name: A Small Inlet

desc:
The mild sounds of crashing waves bounce endlessly off these cavern walls,
mixing and blending into a cacophony of echos. Large drops of mountain chilled
water fall from the ceiling, splashing the icy lake water into your face and
eyes. Never touched by the light of Anar, Eru only knows what could live here.
name: Dark Lake

desc:
The mild sounds of crashing waves bounce endlessly off these cavern walls,
mixing and blending into a cacophony of echos. Large drops of mountain chilled
water fall from the ceiling, splashing the icy lake water into your face and
eyes. Never touched by the light of Arien, Eru only knows what could live here.
name: Dark Lake

desc:
Cold water bites into your legs and ankles, sending a quick shiver up your
spine. Scanning ahead, you try to determine if this is simply an underground
stream, or the beginning of a lake. If it is a lake, swimming it in seems most
foolish, who knows what creatures could lurk within it.
name: A Cave

desc:
Built as a resting area for the Pass, Orcs and Goblins can assemble here without
bottling up the thoroughfare. Large granite boulders offer support for those
simply waiting, and a small trickle of cold mountain water from a underground
mountain stream offers water to parched and sticky lips.
name: A Cave

desc:
Dark and obscure, the walls around you are not easily defined. Your footsteps
announce your presence to anyone nearby, as they crunch and crack over the thin
limestone spears jutting awkwardly from the floor beneath. The sound of water
and cold slimy splash of stilled pools at your feet, indicate water must be
nearby.
name: A Cave

desc:
Carrying you on without purpose or destination, these natural caverns bend west
and south. Faintly pungent in the air, the smell of water and fish, perhaps
resting ahead lies some of the same water that once forged these tunnels.
name: A Cave

desc:
Naturally built by the forces of underground streams and time, these smooth
porous walls are shaped in large elliptical passages. Massive natural archways
lead into underground lairs, where beasts who've never seen the glow of Arien
dwell.
name: A Cavern

desc:
Strewn about, bits of steel and cloth indicate this area to be some sort of
gathering place. Most likely, this is where the warriors of the gate change
shifts, a crude attempt at enforcing order amongst the relatively orderless
hordes of orcs and goblins invading these hollowed walls.
name: A Cave

desc:
The grey walls curve gently here, racing to meet with the Burrow. Curving both
to the north and east, your vision is obscured in this subterranean gloom.
Guided only by the thick granite walls, you can continue north, breaking away
from the Burrow, or east, speeding you closer with its twin roadway.
name: Goblins' Pass

desc:
Built for silence and stealth, the tunnel continues along a north-south axis.
The walls, cooled by the immense mountain surrounding them, act as a cooling
pump, mingling with the stale imprisoned air to create a mild refreshing breeze.
The smooth curved walls around you reflect your footsteps and send them echoing
down into the darkness ahead.
name: Goblins' Pass

desc:
Hard black granite stones form this small beach. Sheltered by the jagged rocks
encircling it, it has been protected from most of the water, a main reason why
it still exists. Crunching the stones beneath your feet, you search for any
signs of activity here, but none can be found.
name: A Rocky Cove

desc:
The mild sounds of crashing waves bounce endlessly off these cavern walls,
mixing and blending into a cacophony of echos. Large drops of mountain chilled
water fall from the ceiling, splashing the icy lake water into your face and
eyes. Never touched by the light of Anar, Eru only knows what could live here.
name: Dark Lake

desc:
The familiar sound of crashing waves is noticeable ahead. Looking downwards, the
calcium-limestone floor abruptly ends, roughly 10 centimeters down from the lip
along the rock, water laps against the impasse. Throwing your light in front of
you, it is reflected along the tiny waves of this underground lake.
name: A Cave

desc:
Large craggy rocks hang from the ceiling and up through the floors. While made
of a soft limestone, their jagged spears could easily disembowel anyone foolish,
or unlucky enough to trip upon them. Collecting in small pockets around the
scarred ground are dark pools of black water, you feel it wise not to drink from
these shallow stagnant pools.
name: A Cavern

desc:
The sharp smell of rotting fish flares your nostrils and constricts your throat.
These walls, worn naturally by the erosion of underground streams, head further
into the darkness, perhaps to the source that first shaped them.
name: A Tunnel

desc:
Narrowing the distance between the two twin roads, the passage cuts southwards
again. Those travelling to the far end of the Burrow, a sharp westwards turn
hastily adds further rock between the two serpentine tunnels.
name: Goblins' Pass

desc:
This open cavern leads to the east, and southwards it connects to the Burrow. A
natural cavern, large stalactites hang precariously from the eroded ceiling.
Equally, jagged stalagmites rest dangerously along the floors. The entire effect
is like walking into the mouth of a snake; one false misstep could send a
hurling spear of rock piercing through your body.
name: A Tunnel Mouth

desc:
Heading sharply east-west, the passage shadows its twin, the Burrow. Sheltered
by meters of hard, thick granite, not even a herd of stampeding Oliphaunts could
send tremors into the Burrow. Lined against the walls, black warg furs lie
draped to muffle any sounds, an added but unnecessary precaution.
name: Goblins' Pass

desc:
As smooth as worn glass, these fleshy grey walls bear you straight ahead.
Travelling along an east-west axis, the worn floor carries you with ease. A
passage leading to the north used to open here, heading towards a maze of
natural caverns and tunnels; however, a massive, unmoveable boulder now seals
the entrance, barring anything larger than a bat from entering.
name: Goblins' Pass

desc:
Checking against the Burrow, this secret route follows the curve of the Burrow,
dipping to the south and west. Stretching your arm span from tip to fingertip,
you cannot reach these walls, nor clearly see the ceiling. Focusing your gaze,
your sight becomes lost in the sea of darkness surrounding you. The solid grey
walls serve as your only guide.
name: Goblins' Pass

desc:
Like a slithering snake, the passageway curves its way through this rocky
mountain. Bending to the east and north, it continues to shadow the mighty
Burrow, trailing it like an invisible predator. To the west, one of the goblin
doors stands, protecting against entry. It serves as an exit for those traveling
along Goblins' Pass.
name: Goblins' Pass

desc:
This dark tunnel serves to confuse those travelling along the Burrow. Seeming to
start from no where, the only exit is westwards, to an opened cavern. Using an
old miner's trick, you gently glide your fingers along the wall, allowing them
to act as a guide while you travel along its curvy length.
name: A Tunnel

desc:
Crafted by the most highly skilled goblins, this pass secretly shadows the
Burrow. Wide and smooth, insulated from the main throughfare by meters of heavy
rock, legions of orcs and goblins could march from here to Wolf's Gate
untraceable to those on the Burrow. Built to ambush and confuse intruders along
the Burrow, this wide tunnel leads to the north and south.
name: Goblins' Pass

desc:
The sulphurous rocks and dust begin to irritate your lungs, stifling back a
cough you rub your red, watering eyes. Every step you take worsens the air
around you, safe if you cannot move, the constant trampling and motion stirs the
long sleeping sulphur dust into the air, making you gag.
name: A Cavern

desc:
The taste of spoiled eggs invades your mouth, and burns your nostrils. The
sulphur is very heavy here, so much that the air begins to resemble a thick
yellow fog. The passage may continue ahead, but you cannot see clearly enough to
tell. For now, the only choice is back to the east.
name: A Cave

desc:
Here the main volume of the slow moving river flowed. Pounding through the soft
limestone, it encountered thick stubborn granite checking its advance to the
east. Splaying outwards with what little fury it possessed, it forged both to
the north and south. Back to the west, lies its now lost source of origin.
name: A Cave

desc:
The ancient river must have been travelling with very little strength. Here, the
walls and ceiling begin to close, shrinking in diameter and height. The
limestone ends abruptly, replaced by noxious sulphur that tickles and constricts
your throat. The passage continues along a north-south tangent.
name: A Tunnel

desc:
Putting more rock between you and the Burrow, this tunnel continues along a
north-south line. Crafted by the most skilled goblins available, its smooth
walls and wide berth allow the passage of companies of orcs simultaneously.
Contained within these walls, could be the march of a thousands boots, echoing
but inescapable along these walls.
name: Goblins' Pass

desc:
Several crushed skeletons lie on the bottom of this dried up well. The well has
become a mass grave for the previous citizens of this destroyed village. A few
stones of the well ring have fallen down into here and crushed the pitiful
corpses further. The walls of the well are rather rugged making it possible to
climb out.
name: Bottom of the Well

desc:
This path has not been used for a very long time. It leads west into the opening
in the side of the mountain. Just outside the opening, where water and mud meet,
there are some very big and deep footprints. From time to time the path is
totally flooded so one has to swim.
name: Flooded Path

desc:
You are in a small cave room just inside the mountain. You hear a lot of
grumbling noises from the large well-lit cave to the west. The cave seems to
have been a mine once, bearing the marks of picks and being quite accurately cut
out in the mountain. However, you cannot believe that the skilled masons who
once made this cave still live here. Definitely, something else has taken this
cave as its home. There is a lot of water on the ground, slowly flowing from the
north to the east.
name: Cave

desc:
You feel a terrible stench from the piles of debris lying in the corners of the
cave. This is the home of big and ugly cave trolls, which are among the most
fearsome creatures in this part of Middle-earth. Scattered around are the
remnants of unlucky animals, humans, dwarves, elves and even orcs upon whom the
trolls have feasted. The only way out seems to be through the large opening to
the east.
name: Trolls' Lair

desc:
Dull grey rock forms the walls of the hidden grotto. Some loose stones lie on
the ground here and there. The cave is almost clean and footprints can be seen
on the floor. The one who lives here might feel some kind of responsibility,
since he obviously tries to avoid making this place a total mess.
name: Trollmother's Grotto

desc:
A thick wall of dark and dense vegetation blocks your way south and west. A tall
tree spreads its branches very high above your head. All is very silent. A damp
smell fills the air. There used to be another tree taller than the other trees,
but someone cut it down and even digged out the root. Are dwarves still around?
The only remains is a waterfilled pit.
name: Thick Forest

desc:
You have found an old, long abandoned mining tunnel. The ceiling is rather low,
forcing tall creatures to bend down to be able to move. The tunnel leads east
and upwards deeper into the mountain. There is a lot of water on the ground in
this part of the shaft.
name: Cave

desc:
A long time ago silver and iron were gathered here to be loaded on mounts and
taken away. Now the cave is empty, and it feels strange to have a place like
this deep inside the mountain.
name: Abandoned Storage Cave

desc:
Different kinds of bushes cover this smooth plain, which is south of a mud-
covered trail. A few small rocks lie across the exposed surface and some earth-
worms are wriggling on the muddy surface. The land continues to be covered by
bushes to the west but turns grassy in the east.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
The plains here are very soft, covered by numerous large bushes growing close
enough to each other to conceal the ground with their giant leaves. A collapsed
guard tower with blackened walls rises to the west where it stands next to a
burned out village. A muddy trail to the north passes near here and comes to an
end in front of the ruined village. This bushy region continues eastward.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
The collapse of most of the supporting walls, the broken ladders, and the
dilapidated ceiling of this wrecked tower makes reaching the upper floor nearly
impossible. The floor is littered with blackened bricks that are highlighted by
several white bones that stick out from underneath. A massive, but worn-out
stonewall can be seen through the hole in the western wall.
name: Collapsed Guard Tower

desc:
The vegetation here is very patchy, with only small areas of plants which are
mostly grass. A few small withered-looking bushes have managed to take root in
the surrounding area. To the north-east is the gateway to a burned down village
and a collapsed guard tower can be seen to the east.
name: Patchy Ground

desc:
Many flat rocks cover this plain which appears very barren with almost no
vegetation. Only a few tussocks of dry grass can be seen growing from the
surface. The ground has fewer stones to the north where a dry-looking plain can
be seen. Farther to the north several mountains loom.
name: Stony Plain

desc:
Peaks of swaying stems of grass lie like a blanket over the fields. Beneath, a
rich earth feeds the vegetation. Smells of herbs and dirt, and a sense of soft
moisture fill the air.
name: Grazing Fields

desc:
In all directions the green grass keeps a firm grip on the land. Far in the
north-west horizon however the peaks of mountains can be spotted by the keen eye
gazing down through mists and clouds. In this area though there is little sight
of slopes or peaks, and this form stretches far away in the distance.
name: Thriving Swards

desc:
Looking out over the fields rolling away into the horizon offers a sight only
comparable to an ocean. The colours of the ocean have been switched from the
marine blue to green and brown, but still undulate and shift like waves on a
frantic sea. The earth beneath the grass is soft and even soggy in places, but
solid enough to not give way when rain and snow falls.
name: Flourishing Lea

desc:
Moisture and wealth of growth dominates the fields. The level landscape is
covered with a thick, swaying coat of straws in a vast range of shapes and
colours. This area serves as home, playground and pantry for its inhabitants.
The earth is soft and rich, and fills the air with a smell of soft dirt.
name: Prosperous Green

desc:
The carpet of grass and plants stretches over the landscape, shifting from the
lightest shades of brown, to sharp, almost luminous hues of green. As the old
plants decay they are replaced by fresh crops of grass, forming layers of either
seasoned or keen stems. The decaying smell fills the air, mixing with the
piquant scent of new growing.
name: Lush Grasslands

desc:
The fields are coloured in green and brown, shifting and flowing through the
landscape. Below in the nestled floor of decaying brown grass, ants and other
hard working insects rearrange the miniature scenery as they see fit. To the
south a sharp ridge quickly climbs and reaches its top. Beyond this high ground
sun-burnt grass and brown reeds quickly replace the lush greens. The ridge is
covered in rocks and gravel, serving as home for numerous insects and small
reptiles. To the east gravel and a large stone has formed a shelter protecting
the ground from wind and rain.
name: Beneath a Steep Ridge

desc:
The luscious cover of grass fills the air with scents of herbs and dirt. The
plain stretches out and touches the horizon in all directions. To the south a
steep slope quickly works its way up to an impassable sharp top. On the ascent
the plants and grass crave the sun, but their location on the north-faced slope
only grants their wish during a few short hours every day. To the west large
stones and gravel fallen from the side of the slope effectively block the path.
name: Luscious Greens at a Narrow Crest

desc:
The ever flowing green is abruptly interrupted here by long since overgrown
squares of pasture. They are framed with what once could be called ditches in a
pattern far too symmetrical to be made by nature itself. The ground is level and
the elsewhere so mandatory gravel and small rocks are nowhere to be seen. The
shallow ditches are soggy and overgrown; apparently rain still gathers in their
lines and a thirsty wanderer will be able to find enough here to dampen his
need. In the south a sharp ridge cuts through the land, creating a grey scar of
rocks and gravel in the fields.
name: Old Pastures at a Steep Ridge

desc:
Small squares of fields, regulated by shallow overgrown ditches, break the
flowing dominance of grass and herbs. While the stems of green and brown still
cover the landscape, here it has been ordered into tidy shapes. It is obvious
that these pastures has long since been abandoned, but they nevertheless still
serve as a haven for more craving plants and herbs, with rich earth and gravel
free ground. In the grass-filled trenches, water is collected as rain falls.
name: An Overgrown Pasture

desc:
Swaying grass, layered with brown and green in different heights and
appearances, rules the land. To the west a more ordered line of fields can be
seen, as if placed there by hand, but here the unordered scheme of nature takes
its grasp of the earth again. A gentle slope stretches up in the south, and
beyond its face more dried out scenery can be seen.
name: Spreading Fields

desc:
This field is covered with golden grass reaching to the knees of a full-grown
man, and in places even higher. The straws grow closer to each other here than
in the surrounding areas, swaying when the wind blows as golden waves in the
grass. Every footstep here is followed by a small cloud of dust, disturbed by
the movement.
name: Thirsty Hays

desc:
Close to the ground the golden straws of grass create a labyrinth in the dust
and gravel. The earth is cracked in places, and serves as home for numerous
insects and small reptiles. In the north greener fields resembling pastures
cover the land, while in the southwest a stony ledge stubbornly cuts the land.
name: Gravelled Earth

desc:
This field flourishes in its own richness; the land is alive with animals using
it as home and hunting ground. An occasional bird might be spotted, casting its
shadow on the green grass, along with a quick rabbit running through the stems,
or a snake hiding its presence among the gravel. To the north the landscape
shades in gold and yellow, and in the south the land is sickened with the
excessive supply of water.
name: Opulent Grasslands

desc:
In the north a steep ridge cuts through the landscape and continues westwards
while it in the east gently levels out with the landscape. It lets this area
keep the rain that falls, and the grass can offer itself some shade during early
and late hours of the day. While the land further in the south becomes soggy and
damp, this area keeps a good balance and the green can flourish. The smell of
herbs and flowers fills the air, but for the keen nose a scent of fungi and
wetness flows in from the south.
name: Rich Greens

desc:
Yellow and golden straws spread like a carpet over the fields. To the south and
east their richness increases into a far greener appearance. Water running from
the cracked earth in the north makes the land in the south wet and misty. Gazing
even further the contours of the Gladden Fields paint their dark shapes on the
horizon.
name: Dust Filled Acres

desc:
Dirt and dust flows through the air, reflecting any light from sun or moon as
countless fire-flies. In the northeast a sharp, prominent ridge runs over the
fields. The northern side is steep and rocky, while the southern side slopes
gently downwards, placing the thirsting plants in the cross-line of the sun,
parching their presence.
name: Parched Slope in the Plains

desc:
In the north a sharp ridge runs from east to west, filled with hazardous rocks
and gravel. Beyond it rich green fields stretch out into the horizon, but here
the earth is harder and the surface is covered with dust and gravel between the
yellow straws of grass. The busy insects are ever present, but the sound flowing
in the air seems damp and lazy.
name: Dry Slope

desc:
Climbing a daring crest to the north, the green grass turns into a mesh of dry
straws, sloping down into the south. The slant is sharp and mostly noticeable by
the occasional shifting of rocks on the slope. There is nothing here that
provides shelter from the sun, and the rain that falls quickly flows away on the
hard earth.
name: On a Crest in the Fields

desc:
The earth is dry and dusty, but in the south where the slope levels out into the
rest of the land, the intense green colours of fresh grass reclaim their hold of
the scene. Small flowers and plants fight for their presence among the ruling
grass, offering a change of colour in the otherwise so sectarian hues.
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
The slope in the north gently evens out here, allowing the downpour to feed the
thirsty plants. Crevices and lines in the moist earth lead muddy water
southwards, into a mossy and mist-filled landscape. The swaying hay in these
fields seems to enjoy the mixture of wetness and sun, and exhibits it in its
green splendour.
name: Green Splendour

desc:
While green hay and small yellow flowers still grow in rows, the earth here has
become soggy and moist. Moss springs up, covering the ground between the straws,
colouring the landscape in a hue of dusky green.
name: Mossy Fields

desc:
This peninsula shoots out into the marshes, serving as a last stand for the
green hays that flow in from the north. It ends abruptly into the Gladden Fields
in the south, east and west. On the border of the marshes, a grey kind of grass
stands tall in the moss and water, as if watching over the fields.
name: Peninsula through the Marshes

desc:
Stretching in from the north the edge of a large peninsula cuts into the Gladden
Fields. Its body is spotted with dots of yellow flowers and patches of green
hay, but as it descends into the moss and muddy waters only grey stems of grass
still stand.
name: Peak of a Peninsula

desc:
The dry slanting ground levels out with the green fields to the south. A large
rock covered in dust and gravel, but with little signs of moss and lichen, has
seemingly scrambled down on the long slope and come to a rest here, among golden
straws and crushed gravel. The hay has embraced the boulder and crawled half-way
up its sides.
name: Rock on the Dried Descent

desc:
Descending down a rather narrow opening between the earth and the foot of the
rock, a damp space opens up. Its sides are dug out from the invigorating soil,
and the dirty rock serves as a hard and stable ceiling. Along the walls small
cavities have been dug out and in them decaying carcasses of small animals rot
away, instigating a strong odour which makes the air heavy to breathe. On the
floor straws of hay and moss have been laid, creating a resting place for the
residents.
name: Damp Den beneath the Fields

desc:
As the descent from the north evens out into a wide field, the ground can once
again claim its need for moist and sun. The surrounding fields are covered in
high green hay, so common for this area. Small flowers spread along the ground
letting patches of hay sway among them.
name: Field of Flowers and Hay

desc:
The green plains of the north are quickly covered with moss and moisture here.
The hay still stands tall, swaying silently, but the rows of stems have grown
thinner, and the mossy ground beneath is clearly visible. A decaying stench
flows in from the south, almost visible to the eye, as it lies as a blanket over
the fields.
name: Soggy Fields of Hay

desc:
At the western side of this part of the marshes, a thick row of sturdy grey
grass grows for miles and miles. They seem to protect the marshes from the
piercing peninsula in the west, which cuts in among the moss and lichen. The
soggy ground continues fearlessly in the east and the south firmly covering the
land with its wetness. To the north a small slopes climbs into the fields above.
name: Steep Sides of a Cape

desc:
The thick rows of green grass flowing in from the north spread out in thinner
and thinner patches as the field rolls southwards. The ground is spotted with
patches of moss and lichen, which grow thicker as the borders of the Gladden
Fields approach. A small slope separates these wetlands from the marsh in the
south.
name: Sloping Wetlands

desc:
Patches of green and brown grass flow over the earth, flowing towards the
horizon. From the north a gentle slope descends to these fields. The fragile
contours of solitary trees spread over the lands; they are drawn in black and
grey on the canvas of the sky. Patches of white flowers spring up here and
there, claiming a scrap of land as their own.
name: Level Fields

desc:
Small yellow flowers spot the mossy ground, and among them high swaying stems of
green grass stand proudly. The ground has however become soft and moss-filled
compared to the lands in the north, and neither flowers nor grass can grow to
any significant stature, as their roots rot away in the wet ground. The border
of the Gladden Fields quickly ends the chance of further growth in the south.
Even further away the dark marshes reach out, covered in mist.
name: Along the Border of Gladden Fields

desc:
Grass in hundreds of hues of green and brown embraces the soft earth. The
landscape flows gently in all directions, undulating towards the horizon. In the
north and west the fields continue in this fashion as far the eye can see. In
the opposite directions, a gentle mist is starting to show over the fields,
connecting with the Gladden Fields in the south, and the mighty Anduin far in
the east.
name: Low Plains

desc:
As the ground slopes into the south rainfall and moisture from the fields in the
north seeps through these fields, leaving them soft and wet. Moss has started to
spring up between the ever growing rows of green grass, and gentle yellow
flowers have found a place to flourish. The air is filled with a gentle mist,
and from the south a distinct smell of wet and rotting fields flows in.
name: Misty Fields

desc:
A sparse growth of orange grass is bravely fighting for this piece of land. The
moss-filled ground fights back, rendering the ground soggy and too wet for the
fragile roots of the grass. In this battle small yellow flowers have found their
way to grow beside the moss, sending their stems down in the moist earth. In the
southeast the fields fall down into a sink where dark marshes grab a hold in the
land.
name: Cornered Moist Fields

desc:
Row upon row of thick grey grass covers the border of the Gladden Fields in the
north and a sharp edge of turf and grass cuts into the marsh in the east. Behind
this wall of grey, a sharp descent reaches down into the moss-filled mire. The
marshes consist of patches of undulating moss and lichen, travelling up and down
in shallow muddy waters, stretching miles at end.
name: Embarking the Gladden Fields

desc:
The marshes reach out in the south, covered in a firm mist ascending from the
soggy ground. Soft moss flows over the marshes in patches, enclosed by ponds of
grey, muddy water. A damp odour of decay fills the air. To the north the sharp
slippery shore of the ancient Gladden makes passing impossible.
name: Outer Shores of the Gladden Fields

desc:
Grey moss and lichen crawls over the lands, up and down through sinks of water,
ever continuing southwards, stretching out into the horizon. A shallow mist
covers this display with its foggy appearance, rising from the wet ground. In
the north the mosses come to a stop as the Gladden Fields border to the green
fields above.
name: Descent into the Gladden Fields

desc:
The moss-covered sloping sides of the peninsula in the northwest quickly descend
into the water-filled marches. This must have been a shallow part of the ancient
lake, but a few hundred feet further out it levels out with the rest of the
soggy ground. Row upon row of grey grass grows along the border of the cape,
spotted with small yellow flowers. Turfs of lower grass and decaying bushes
decorate the sides of the mossy patches in the swamp.
name: Mossy Edge of a Peak

desc:
Descending from the isle in the east, the moss here sinks low into the ground,
so low that the pools of water connect and create a small lake in the marsh.
There is no telling how deep it might be; the muddy water renders seeing the
bottom impossible, but a daring diver might find it further down than expected.
The small lake stretches out into the south. In the north and west the mossy
ground quickly levels with the surrounding land, while in the east there is a
steep drop trickled with small currents from the marshes.
name: Deep Pool in the Marsh

desc:
A shallow isle rises from the soft moss and muddy waters. Compared to the
surrounding areas the ground is firmer and even dry at places. The dense mist of
the mire does not reach to here, but it encircles the base of the ascent. Sharp
slopes descend in the north and west, while the promontory stretches further in
the east and south.
name: Incline in the Marsh

desc:
A trifling of a hill reaches up from the soggy marsh. It is surrounded by lower
pools of water and floating moss, but up here grey and, in some places, even
green straws of grass sway obliviously. At the borders of the incline, sturdy
thick grass with a sharp grey tone carefully watches the deceiving mire around.
The hill stretches out in the west and south, but declines back into the marsh
in the north and east.
name: Mossy Hill in the Gladden Fields

desc:
Behind a small wooden gate a chamber opens up, larger than one might expect from
the outside. Inside the walls are dark and crude, and little attention has been
given to details and perfection. As a matter of fact the large rocks and other
debris seem to barely hold together, but serve as the needed shelter it was
created for. Along one side of the room a roaring fire burns with an intense
glow fed by large beams of wood and the many holes in walls, letting air flow
through it. At the opposite side of the fire a flimsy ladder leads up through a
hole in the rocky ceiling. Straight ahead a small opening of which half is dug
in the earth, leads down a short slope further into the hovel.
name: Entering a Damp Chamber

desc:
A few blankets, worn and filthy, are spread over the dirty floor. The walls in
this room are mostly made of the dirt of the very earth. Only close to the rocky
ceiling stones and debris form the protection from the fields above. There is a
rigid stench flowing in the air, and the room is cold and damp, reminding more
of a cellar than a living quarter. In one of the corners a heavy chest has been
placed.
name: Filthy Living Quarters

desc:
A ladder reaches from the chamber below, and after several yards the narrow
tunnel arrives at a small crude watch tower. On either sides of the hole created
by the tunnel a few feet wide ledge serves as an outpost for watching the lands.
The ledge is enclosed by rocky walls reaching up a yard or two, serving as
protection from wind and rain. The ceiling consists of a sloping hoard of debris
created in such a manner that it protects more from the burning sun than falling
rain. Remains of numerous meals lie spread around the opening, telling about the
countless hours spent on guard here.
name: A Crude Watchtower

desc:
Descending from the murky surface the water becomes cold and the visibility
extends only a few yards away in the muddy lake. The pool is surprisingly deep,
and close to the mossy bottom no sunlight can reach from the marsh above.
name: Bottom of the Murky Lake

desc:
This mining tunnel was built by dwarves long time ago. The tunnels to north and
south are caved in and the only options are to go back or continue eastwards.
The wall to the north is beginning to crumble, and clear, cold water gushes
forth from cracks in the stone.
name: Deserted Mine Shaft

desc:
The water rises now, reaching almost to your knees. Fighting a swift current,
the icy water numbs your legs, just when you need them the most to secure your
footing. Scanning ahead, it is clear that the water level isn't receding, this
is a dead end.
name: A Cave
